turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: I join the court's opinion in full. Although it is not my practice to announce my separate opinions from the bench, the race based discrimination against Asian-Americans in these cases compels me to do so today. In the wake of the Civil War, our nation amended our constitution to abolish slavery. It further proclaimed that all persons born in the United States are citizens entitled to the privileges or immunities of citizenship and the equal protection of the laws. As Justice Harlan proclaimed in dissent in Plessy versus Ferguson, our constitution is color blind and neither knows nor tolerates classes among citizens. The great failure and tragedy of this country was slavery and its progeny, and the great failure of this court was its misinterpretation of the reconstruction amendments and its rejection of Justice Harlan's view in Plessy, thereby sanctioning decades of racial discrimination and Jim Crow Laws. For the past two decades now, this court has permitted universities to maintain race conscious admission programs under a framework set forth in Grutter versus Bollinger. The court's opinion today rightly makes clear that Grutter now, for all intents and purposes, is overruled. The court sees the universities' admissions policies for what they are, race based preferences designed to ensure a particular racial mix in their classes. These policies fly in the face of our color blind constitution and our nation's equality ideal. Throughout the debates of Thirteenth and Fourteenth Amendments as well as the landmark civil rights acts of the era, proponents repeatedly affirmed their belief in equal citizenship and racial equality. Senator Sumner, a leading proponent of the Fourteenth Amendment echoed the point in debating the Civil Rights Act of 1875, remarking that treating anyone differently based on their skin color is plain oppression, which he said, you would feel keenly what directed against you or your child. Petitioner here represents Asian-American college applicants who allege that at the margins Asian applicants are harmed, denied admission to the respondent universities because of their race. Such discrimination is plainly and boldly unconstitutional. As an initial matter, Asian-Americans cannot be described as the beneficiaries of historical racial advantages. To the contrary, our nation's first immigration ban targeted the Chinese. In later years, strong anti-Asian sentiments in the western states led to many discriminatory laws at the state and local levels, similar to those aimed at blacks in the south. Segregation in public facilities, including schools, was quite common until after the Second World War. Also, following the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor, Japanese-Americans in the American west were evacuated and interned in relocation camps. Yet Harvard and UNC now forthrightly state that they racially discriminate when it comes to admitting students. They argue that their discrimination is a good thing that ought to continue indefinitely, and they encourage the court to look pass discrimination against Asian applicants and focus instead on the supposed benefits of race conscious admission programs for blacks and Hispanics. But the constitution embodies a simple truth. Two discriminatory wrongs cannot make a right. We cannot allow race conscious measures to continue simply because we think that such arrangements are superior to the constitution. Given the history of discrimination against Asian-Americans, especially their history with segregated schools, it seems particularly ironic and wrong to suggest that a past history of segregationist policies toward blacks should be remedied at the expense of Asian-American applicants today. Regardless, the constitution abhors classifications based on race. Those classifications may actually harm favored races or be based on hidden and illegitimate motives, and more fundamentally, every time the government places citizens on racial registers and makes race relevant to the provision of burdens of benefits, it demeans us all. Even focusing on the supposed benefits of racial preferences, history has repeatedly shown that purportedly benign discrimination may be pernicious, and that discriminators may go to great lengths to hide and perpetuate their unlawful conduct. Slaveholders once argued that slavery was a positive good, that civilized blacks have elevated them in every dimension of life. Segregationist similar asserted that segregation was not only benign but good for black students. Again and again, racially burdensome programs have been justified as helping the every groups they oppress. Yet the Fourteenth Amendment outlaws government sanctioned racial discrimination of all types. In fact, in the zero sum game of college admissions, it is not even theoretically possible to help a certain racial group without causing harm to members of other racial groups. Every time the government uses racial criteria purporting to bring the races together, someone gets excluded, like the Asian-American students here, and the person excluded suffers an injury solely because of his or her race. So anyone who defends these programs must be able to tell hardworking young people exactly why they have been penalized because of the color of their skin. This is not 1958 or 1968. Today's 17-year-olds after all did not live through the Jim Crow era. They did not enact or enforce segregation laws or take any action to oppress or enslave the victims of the past. Whatever their skin color, today's youth are simply not responsible for the segregation that many of us suffer during the 20th Century. They do not shoulder the moral deaths of their ancestors. Far from improving race relations in our nation, racial preferences highlight our racial differences with pernicious effect. The racial boxes into which universities place applicants are little more than stereotypes suggesting that immutable characteristics somehow conclusively determine a person's ideology, beliefs and abilities. That is decidedly false and dehumanizing. People who share a similar skin color may have entirely different experiences and viewpoints. The same skin color does not mean that we look alike or think alike. To link individuals by only their skin color is racialism itself, and racialism simply cannot be undone by different or more racialism. If we continue down this road, who can say what future groups will claim discrimination from today's policies while Asian-Americans of the future require race discrimination in their favor to remedy today's injustices. The Fourteenth Amendment explicitly cut off such never ending cycles of victimization. Instead the solution announced in the second founding is incorporated in our constitution that we're all equal and should be treated equally before the law without regard to our race. This is not the 1860s or the 1960s with widespread government imposed racial policies dividing our nation. Whatever remedial measures may have been constitutionally permitted back then are no longer permissible today. Contrary to today's narrative, we are not all trapped in a fundamentally racist society. The original sin of slavery and the historically subjugation of black Americans do not still determine our lives today. To believe otherwise would lock us into a seemingly perpetual inferior caps. This is irrational. It is an insult to individual achievement and cancerous to young minds. If an applicant has less financial means, then surely a university may take that into account, just as it may take into account an applicant's ability to overcome any other difficult circumstance. What the university cannot do is use the applicant's skin color as a heuristic, assuming that because he is black, he therefore conforms to the university's simplistic view of an abstract average black person, nor can universities favor certain race over Asian-Americans in their admissions decisions. Today, tomorrow the popular and unpopular races may well change places. The equal citizenship guarantee codified in the Fourteenth Amendment made us all citizens of one nation, governed by one constitution. The court today lives up to the promise of the second founding and ensures that the promise of equal citizenship continues to be fulfilled.
